paper_title: Application of GIS and logistic regression to fossil pollen data in modelling present and past spatial distribution of the Colombian savanna

methods.description: Describes the use of Local Polynomial and Radial Basis Functions interpolation techniques to create spatial surfaces from measured points (likely pollen data, based on the paper title). Applied using the Geostatistical Analyst within ArcGIS.

steps.description: In the Local Polynomial interpolation, a power of two is used and the weight distance optimised.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Local Polynomial interpolation with specified parameters.

steps.description: Interpolated values less than zero were omitted from the analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter interpolated surface to remove values below zero.

steps.description: Apply Radial Basis Functions interpolation using the ''multiquadratic function''.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Radial Basis Functions interpolation using the multiquadratic function.

steps.description: There are more parameter decisions [for Radial Basis Functions], which allow a variety of map outputs. This flexibility of the different applications within the interpolation functions requires decision-making.
steps.searchable_summary: Configure parameters for Radial Basis Functions interpolation.

methods.description: Describes the use of various metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, Overall Accuracy, Cohen's Kappa, True Skill Statistic) to evaluate the predictive success of binary ecological models. Calculations are based on a confusion matrix.

steps.description: From a confusion matrix (Table 1) that records the number of (a) true presence, (b) false presence, (c) false absence and (d) true absence, all accuracy measures are calculated (Table 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Create a confusion matrix from observed and predicted binary outcomes.
outputs: Confusion matrix (counts of TP, FP, FN, TN)

steps.description: Sensitivity is the percentage of correctly predicted savanna occurrences to the total number of savanna pixels.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate sensitivity from confusion matrix.

steps.description: Specificity is defined as the proportion of correctly predicted absences of savanna to the total number of absences.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate specificity from confusion matrix.

steps.description: The overall accuracy is defined as the percentage of correctly predicted raster-pixels to the total number of pixels in the area of interest.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate overall accuracy from confusion matrix.

steps.description: Cohen's Kappa coefficient is designed to reflect the models performance in absence and presence simultaneously.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate Cohen's Kappa coefficient from confusion matrix.

steps.description: We also include an alternative measure for the kappa statistic proposed by Allouche et al. (2006), the so-called True skill statistic (TSS). This measure is suitable for models that generate presence-absence predictions.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate True Skill Statistic (TSS) from confusion matrix.

steps.description: To interpret the power of the model calculated by the Kappa and the TSS measurement, the classification by Landis and Koch (1977) [is used].
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret model performance based on Kappa and TSS values using a standard classification scheme.

methods.description: Describes the process of validating the logistic regression model by implementing its outcome (logit equation) in a GIS, generating a predicted land-cover map, and comparing it spatially to the observed distribution.

steps.description: The outcome of the model (logit equation) is introduced into the GIS to let the model run with the related climate layers as a data source.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply logistic regression model equation within a GIS environment.
outputs: Predicted spatial values (e.g., probability of savanna presence)

steps.description: A map of the predicted land-cover distribution is the result of this implementation.
steps.searchable_summary: Create a map from predicted spatial values.
outputs: Predicted land-cover map (e.g., binary presence/absence map derived from probability)

steps.description: This map is subsequently compared to the actually observed spatial distribution of savanna to evaluate how well the model performs.
steps.searchable_summary: Compare predicted spatial distribution map with observed spatial distribution map.

steps.description: By subtracting the layers of the observed and predicted spatial distribution of savanna from one another in the GIS, the accuracy of the model can be illustrated, and a differentiation made between false presence and false absence predictions of savanna.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform spatial subtraction of observed and predicted binary maps in GIS.
outputs: Difference map highlighting agreement, false positives, and false negatives

methods.description: Describes the process of preparing the database by overlaying GIS layers, creating random and regular point rasters, and extracting underlying variable data for modeling.

steps.description: To understand how the variables spatially relate to each other, all layers were overlaid in the GIS.
steps.searchable_summary: Overlay multiple GIS raster or vector layers.

steps.description: A GIS layer was created in which a random point raster was built. [...] the random point raster consisted of a layer of 2000 points scattered out over the area of interest.
steps.searchable_summary: Generate a random point layer within a specified area.

steps.description: For every point, the underlying data of the variable layers was extracted into a DBF-format document.
steps.searchable_summary: Extract raster or vector attribute values at random point locations.
outputs: Table or database file (DBF) containing extracted variable values for each random point

steps.description: a second point layer of evenly spaced grid points was constructed, and the underlying data was extracted in a similar way (''regular sampling'', Hirzel and Guisan 2002). [...] the regular sample layer consisted of a square of 18 · 18 data points.
steps.searchable_summary: Generate a regular grid point layer within a specified area.

steps.description: the underlying data was extracted in a similar way [as random points].
steps.searchable_summary: Extract raster or vector attribute values at regular grid point locations.
outputs: Table or database file (DBF) containing extracted variable values for each regular grid point

steps.description: The land-cover layer has been converted to a presence/absence layer of savanna.
steps.searchable_summary: Convert a categorical land cover layer into a binary presence/absence layer for a specific class.

steps.description: the outcomes of the descriptive statistics each form a separate GIS layer.
steps.searchable_summary: Create GIS layers from descriptive statistics results.
outputs: Separate GIS layers representing descriptive statistics outcomes
